text: Roboticohepaticojaygenostomy for a common bile duct injury.
A forty-year-old woman with a duodenal ulcer who underwent an open antrectomy and Billy Roth I reconstruction.
On post-op day ten, found to be jaundice, CT scan demonstrated a stapled common bile duct.
A percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) drain is visibly placed after an interval period of four months.
The robotic hepaticojejunostomy procedure is performed, visually connecting the hepatic duct to the jejunum using robotic surgical instruments.
The surgical team begins by carefully taking down adhesions, likely using instruments to separate and remove fibrous tissue connections within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and divides the jejunum approximately twenty centimeters distal to this anatomical landmark.
The surgeon brings the proximal jejunum up through the transverse mesocolon, positioning it for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to bring the jejunum and another structure together, creating a J-Gynostomy.
The surgeon closes the common enterotomy using a IV-O V-lock suture on a CV-XXIII needle, applying a continuous suturing technique.
The surgeon performs hilar dissection, carefully separating and exposing the structures within the hilum.
The surgeon mobilizes the stuck duodenum and lowers the hilar plate, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon identifies and mobilizes the gallbladder, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, ensuring clear identification of key anatomical landmarks, before performing the cholecystectomy to remove the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to verify the placement of a stent within the common bile duct, displaying the stent's position and alignment within the duct.
The surgeon opens the duct, revealing its interior structure and facilitating further surgical access.
The surgeon reaches the bifurcation, where the PTC stent is visibly positioned within the ductal structures.
The surgeon elevates the jejunum to the bifurcation and creates a matching enterotomy.
The surgeon observes the opening in the duct, which measures approximately two centimeters in diameter.
The surgeon makes a slightly smaller incision in the jejunum, preparing for further surgical steps.
The surgeon constructs a diamond-shaped anastomosis at the six o'clock position in the duct, gradually suturing upwards from both sides to connect the duct and jejunum.
This can be done with a four O V lock as well.
If the duct is smaller, a five O monocryl on RV one can be used, or if even smaller, a six O with a TF needle.
It is not mandatory to tie since we're using a locking stitch.
You can either keep working further on either side to create overlap.
You can tie or you can use a weck.
We did all three techniques in this case to allow the resident to practice.
Here's the overlap.
The resident is tying a suture knot, securing the tissue with a visible locking stitch.
The surgeon places a Weck clip on the surgical site, securing the tissue or vessel as part of the procedure.
The surgeon performs gastric suspension, creating a serosal patch to cover the anastomosis site.
